follow_up_question: Can you think of fun ways to spend $1,000,000 that the song might not mention?

title: Lost at Sea: Henry Hudson's Final Voyage
story: Mutiny struck explorer Henry Hudson's ship in 1611! Angry crew members forced Hudson, his young son, and seven others into a small boat on the vast Hudson Bay. As the mutineers sailed away on June 22, the abandoned group was never seen again. Despite the tragic end, Hudson's earlier discoveries paved the way for future explorers in North America.
activity_question: What happened to Henry Hudson and the others set adrift?
activity_choices: They were rescued by another ship
They built a raft and sailed home
follow_up_question: Why do you think the crew mutinied against Hudson?

title: The Hole Story of Doughnuts
story: Craving a sweet treat? Thank Hanson Gregory! Legend says he created the first ring doughnut in 1847. Before June 22 that year, doughnuts were solid and often undercooked in the middle. Gregory's clever idea to punch out the center solved that problem, giving us the delicious, perfectly-cooked doughnuts we love today.
activity_question: What problem did the ring shape solve for doughnuts?
activity_choices: They were undercooked in the middle
activity_answer: They were undercooked in the middle
follow_up_question: Can you think of other foods with holes that might help them cook better?

title: The Last Shot of the Civil War
story: Boom! A cannon fired in the icy Bering Strait. It wasn't an attack, but a surrender. On June 22, 1865, the CSS Shenandoah, a Confederate ship, fired the very last shot of the American Civil War. This happened far from the battlefields, in the chilly waters between Alaska and Russia. The ship's crew had just learned the war was over and fired to signal they were giving up.
activity_question: What was the purpose of the last shot fired by the CSS Shenandoah?
follow_up_question: Why do you think the last shot of the Civil War happened so far from the main battlefields?

title: Bouncing into History: Tennis Takes Root
story: Thwack! A rubber ball soars across a grassy lawn. In 1874, Welsh Major Walter Clopton Wingfield introduced a game that would change sports forever. On June 22, he unveiled lawn tennis, featuring a bouncy rubber ball perfect for grass courts. This exciting new sport quickly caught on, laying the groundwork for the tennis we know and love today.
activity_question: Who introduced the game of lawn tennis?
bonus_fact: The rubber ball was a key innovation, allowing for better bounces on grass than earlier tennis balls.

title: A Royal Eye on the Sky
story: King Charles II had his head in the stars! In 1675, he founded the Royal Greenwich Observatory in England. This special place became the home of astronomy and timekeeping. Scientists there studied the night sky and helped sailors navigate the seas. On June 22, the king's decree set the wheels in motion for this stellar project.
activity_question: What did King Charles II found in 1675?
bonus_fact: Greenwich became the home of the Prime Meridian, the starting point for measuring longitude around the world.

title: Napoleon's Final Bow
story: The mighty emperor Napoleon Bonaparte faced a crushing defeat at Waterloo. On June 22, 1815, he made a big decision. Napoleon stepped down from power for the second time in his life. He handed the crown to his young son, Napoleon II. It was the end of an era for France and all of Europe.
activity_question: Who did Napoleon hand the crown to when he stepped down?
follow_up_question: Why do you think Napoleon chose to step down instead of continuing to fight?

title: A Helping Hand for Heroes
story: Soldiers returning from war faced big challenges. President Franklin Roosevelt had a plan to help. On June 22, 1944, he signed the GI Bill of Rights. This important law gave veterans money for college, help buying homes, and other benefits. It was a way to say 'thank you' for their service and give them a fresh start in civilian life.
activity_question: What did the GI Bill of Rights provide for veterans?
follow_up_question: Why do you think education was an important benefit for returning soldiers?

title: A Heavyweight Battle for the Ages
story: Fists flew as Ezzard Charles and Jersey Joe Walcott clashed in an epic boxing match. On June 22, 1949, at Comiskey Park in Chicago, Charles emerged victorious after 15 grueling rounds. He claimed the National Boxing Association world heavyweight title, cementing his place in boxing history.
activity_question: Where did this famous boxing match take place?
follow_up_question: How many rounds did the boxing match last?

title: Cricket Pioneer Born in Tasmania
story: A groundbreaking cricket player entered the world in Hobart, Australia. Sam Morris, born in 1855, would become the first black player and person of West Indian origin to represent Australia in a Test match. This talented all-rounder's arrival on June 22 marked the beginning of a journey that would help pave the way for diversity in Australian cricket.
activity_question: Where was Sam Morris born?
follow_up_question: What does 'all-rounder' mean in cricket?
